paragraphs.body: An Ever-Growing Problem

The Protagonist looks down the block, seeing the heat waves from the summer sun, and spots a man seemingly in trouble. The man is of a short stature with a badly deformed cleft lip, who is pacing back and forth and what should be murmurs is yelling phrases of agitation. The Protagonist swiftly walks towards the conflict of interest for this story in which it is their duty to solve.

annotations.text: be murmurs is yelling phrases of agitation.

annotations.comment: I enjoy this way of being obvious about the construct of the story. That’s fun!

paragraphs.body: Hey there! What’s your name?

annotations.comment: Is this the Protagonist yelling at the man? Or vice versa?

paragraphs.body: The man pauses for a bit before—

Hey, who’s you? And what’s your freak ass friend doing saying all that garbage? I heards you two from nearly five blocks down! I saws how you talk to sweet Mrs. Smithers and her apple bunch she had in her hand, nearly choked the ol’ lady from boredom!
The man grabs his neck in an almost over the top fashion as he says this, making a goofy face as he does.
Why does lanky boy over here keep describing what I’m doing here? Is he from the ward or something?
The Narrator ignores this comment made by the peculiar smelling man; after giving the Narrator a mean look the Protagonist then brings the conversation back to the matter at hand.

annotations.comment: It might be fun to have the Narrator only describe the man as ugly, etc. once he has insulted the Narrator?

paragraphs.body: Well, we’re here to help with your day! Just as we did for Mrs. Smithers and her apple bunch. She was having a hard time telling which where the granny smiths and which were the golden delicious apples, she almost fainted from the kind act that was bestowed upon her!

Said the Protagonist, with an awfully giddy expression. The Protagonist then elbows the narrator’s side, causing some amount of discomfort. The Narrator goes back to staring at the interaction that’s about to ensue.

annotations.comment: Think through the formatting a bit more. The italicized dialogue is okay, but that makes it hard to use “said the Protagonist” smoothly so perhaps think through that a bit more.

paragraphs.body: See, my overtly observant friend and I want to help you out! You seem to be in some sort of state of trouble, and it’s our job to fix it! So, what issue may you be facing my nameless friend? Speaking of which, what is your name might I ask again?

annotations.text: it’s our job to fix it!
annotations.comment: I tripped up a bit on this - how is it the Protagonist’s job to fix this problem? That’s not what I think of as the Protagonist’s job in a story. You can make it so in THIS story, but that will need unpacked a bit more.

paragraphs.body: The man hesitates, looking slight off putted from the two characters who have just approached him. He then abruptly—

It’s Eddie, Eddie Dingle. That’s right, that’s me! And yea, I have problems. Lot’s of em! But there’s this one problem that’s really been getting to me. See there’s this girl named Nan. Yea? She overpopulated my senses and then dicked me over!
The Protagonist makes a slight grimace. They then clear their throat and respond to what Eddie Dingle just described to them.

annotations.comment: Is the Protagonist annoyed by the appearance of these other characters?

paragraphs.body: Oh, I’m sorry to hear that, Eddie Dingle! How did she um, “dick you over” exactly?
Eddie seems hesitant by the question the Protagonist, staring at the ground almost as if he doesn’t want to answer. Eddie then sharply looks up at the protagonist and—

Hey, stop that horse talk, you weirdo! We can sees I’m struggling here a bit, and you keeps announcing it to the whole block! Anyways miss, yea I saw her walking down the street with this guy named Danny. I hate Danny!

Eddie expresses his hatred towards Danny by lifting his arms up high, revealing the large sweat stains in the pits of his shirt. Eddie immediately puts his arms back down and his hands into his arm pits out of embarrassment, as if he heard someone audibly point out the sweat stains. The Protagonist again turns and sighs at the Narrator, then the Narrator goes back to observing the situation at hand, seeing the Protagonist about to speak.

annotations.comment: I like the idea of those two but am not quite sure what they’re doing in the story yet.

paragraphs.body: Well, what about Danny don’t you like? Is he mean or something like that?
He’s just a fucker that’s it! I deserve Nan. I do!

Eddie boldly announces with a crazed look in his eyes, then quickly glances at the Narrator. The Protagonist appears slightly irked by Eddie’s reply; Eddie then proceeds to roll his eyes by what the Narrator is saying. The Protagonist tries to make Eddie see his ordeal in a new light.

annotations.comment: Another opportunity for the Narrator to kind of comment on this a bit.

paragraphs.body: Well Eddie, have you considered her feelings? Maybe she just simply likes Danny and wants to be with him! Have you ever talked to this Nan girl before?
Well, no. Not really. I was gonna to, but you two gots in my way just as I was about to!
The Protagonist face lifts up with joy while Eddie stares at the Narrator, with a face of slight frustration as he questions what they’re even adding to the conversation.
So, you were going to maybe talk it out with Nan? That’s great! What were you going to say? Maybe how you’re feeling and come up with a mutual agreement?
Well, I wasn’t really gonna talk to her in like the usual way. I was gonna express my thoughts and feelings the Dingle way. Family tradition for when one of us Dingle’s get dicked over!

annotations.comment: I really like the voice of this character (but I am having trouble following how this relates back to the troubled guy at the beginning).

paragraphs.body: The Protagonist makes a face of concern, scared of what the short man has brewing up in his mind.
And what is that exactly?
Well miss, if you may ask. I gots this dog dookie in a bag. To make things fair and square I was gonna rub some of it on my hands and slap that Danny straight for messing with my Nan!

The Protagonist and Narrator’s eyes widen after hearing what Eddie has plotted, a sickening sight. The Protagonist puts her over mouth as they cannot fathom such a plan while the Narrator observes what’s about to—

Yea yea, we get it ya werido! Always observing these things and whatnot. We know! Anyways I think Nan and Danny will be walking down Fifth Corner Street here pretty soon, so I’m gonna gets myself ready. Catch you on the flip, I’m gonna fix all my problems today!

As Eddie Dingle walks off from view of the Protagonist, they’re still stunned by what they just heard. As they stare off blankly they question if he even needed their help to begin with, he seemed to have it all figured out. After a rush of thoughts, the Protagonist sharply turns to the Narrator with optimism in their eyes.

annotations.comment: I wasn’t sure if the Protagonist was the they or if there is more than 1 person here.

paragraphs.body: Well, I guess some people got it all figured out, good for him!
The Narrator nods their head in agreement.
He did say they’d be walking down Fifth Corner Street.. I think there’s another problem to fix over there!
The Narrator again nods their head in agreement. And so, the Protagonist walks towards where Eddie Dingle said Nan and Danny would be walking through, hoping to solve another problem.

annotations.comment: Adeline, I enjoyed this draft. I found it ambitious and winning — I liked the idea (and am a big fan of meta narratives like this one). And the dialogue is a ton of fun. That said, unsurprisingly, it doesn’t quite work yet. I think for me the biggest issue is the set up of this idea — what is the Protagonist to do in the story at the top? And how does she (?) know what she is to do? (Maybe the narrator could tell her?) I think you need a conflict beyond the one she’s there to solve — something the Protagonist wants (to be allowed to go home/to talk to the narrator/to stop being a protagonist) that resolves over the course of the story. Then it won’t matter that the scene we see with Eddie doesn’t actually wrap up in this draft. Also — a little clarity on the formatting would help. I hope that makes sense, but I’m happy to discuss with you if it would help! Happy revising!Happy revising!
